text:
```json
{
  "results": [
    {
      "start_time": 0.0,
      "end_time": 94.0,
      "transcript": "Tonight, breaking news. The jury has reached a partial verdict in the Sean Diddy Combs case. We'll take you inside court. What Combs did when he learned the jury had already reached a verdict on four counts after just two days of deliberating. But the most complex and punitive charge, racketeering, the jury still not unanimous. We're live. Travel nightmares, severe storms, crushing travel along the I-95 corridor, ground delays and ground stops at airports along the East Coast. Thousands of delays nationwide, our inside look at the mad dash to keep millions traveling on time. Plus, beaches shut down across multiple states due to high levels of bacteria. What you need to know to stay safe this week. Alligator Alcatraz, President Trump on hand at the opening of a migrant detention center in the Florida Everglades, greeted today by protesters. Our cameras capturing the alligators, officials say will act as natural deterrence. President Trump's mega bill now goes to the House, the vice president casting the tie-breaking vote. The moment captured by our team highlighting the GOP divide as the Trump and Musk feud erupts again. The president threatening Doge will look into Musk. And a fentanyl bust like you've never seen before. We'll show you the undercover sting to catch Chinese nationals selling the raw chemicals needed to produce the deadly drug. This is NBC Nightly News with Tom Yamas.",
      "topic_subject": "News Broadcast Intro",
      "summary": "Anchor Tom Llamas introduces the top stories for the evening, including the partial verdict in the Sean Diddy Combs trial, severe weather disrupting holiday travel, beach closures due to bacteria, President Trump's tour of a new migrant detention center, the Senate passing a major budget bill, and an exclusive DEA sting operation.",
      "keywords": [
        "Tom Llamas",
        "NBC Nightly News",
        "Diddy trial",
        "travel delays",
        "beach closures",
        "Trump",
        "DEA sting"
      ]
    },
    {
      "start_time": 94.0,
      "end_time": 245.0,
      "transcript": "And good evening. We come on the air with breaking news tonight. A federal jury has reached a verdict on four charges against Sean Diddy Combs, but not all. Now the judge has asked the jury to keep deliberating, but leaving us all to wonder how they decided. The jury indicating the loan charge they could not reach a unanimous verdict on, racketeering. Sean Combs' lawyers circling their client and hugging as the world awaits word of what the jury comprised of eight men and four women decided. But we will have to wait at least until tomorrow morning when they return to deliberate. It's all been rapidly unfolding tonight. We begin with Chloe Malas who was in the courthouse. And Chloe, what did Combs do when the jurors came back so fast with a partial verdict? Tom, that's right. I was in the courthouse watching Combs as he read that latest note from the jury. And like you said, his attorneys circling around him in a very powerful scene. At one point, Combs putting his head in his hands, even rubbing his eyes, looking clearly dejected. We've reached a verdict on counts two, three, four, and five. Read the note from the jury that came just after four o'clock. But we cannot reach a verdict on count one, they wrote, because some jurors have unpersuadable views. That means they've reached a unanimous decision on whether Sean Diddy Combs is guilty of sex trafficking and transportation to engage in prostitution. But the jury of eight men and four women is split on the most serious and complex charge, racketeering conspiracy. For that count, jurors must decide whether they believe Combs ran a criminal organization and committed underlying crimes like kidnapping and arson. Late today, the judge urging the jury to keep deliberating to try to reach a decision on that last count before revealing their verdict. As the jury's note came in, Combs' attorneys hugged and formed a circle around the music mogul who put his head in his hands, at one point giving a thumbs up to his family. During the seven-week trial, prosecutors alleged that the music mogul ran a criminal enterprise and used power, violence and fear to get what he wanted. Calling 34 witnesses, including two of Combs' former girlfriends, Cassie Ventura and another known only as Jane. Combs opting not to testify, his defense team calling no witnesses, telling the jury that the claims against Combs were badly exaggerated and that physical abuse and love of baby oil are not federal crimes. The jury will resume deliberations tomorrow morning. Chloe Malas, NBC News, New York. work.",
      "topic_subject": "Legal/Crime",
      "summary": "A detailed report on the partial verdict in the Sean 'Diddy' Combs trial, where the jury reached a decision on four counts but remains deadlocked on the racketeering conspiracy charge. Correspondent Chloe Malas reports from outside the courthouse on Combs' reaction and the details of the trial.",
      "keywords": [
        "Sean Diddy Combs",
        "trial verdict",
        "jury",
        "sex trafficking",
        "racketeering",
        "Chloe Malas"
      ]
    },
    {
      "start_time": 245.0,
      "end_time": 390.0,
      "transcript": "Also tonight, 26 million are under flooding alerts and 33 million are at risk of severe weather as thunderstorms roll through the northeast, all during one of the summer's busiest travel weeks. NBC's Tom Costello is at Newark Airport as crowds are trying to catch flights tonight. Tom, how's it looking? Yeah, we've got a lot of people who've been waiting a long time to get out of here, but the delays have been building through the day. At the moment, we've got more than 5,700 delays in the United States, 1,300 flight cancellations, and they are extended from Washington, D.C., all the way through New York City. Tuesday evening and day two of severe weather tearing into the I-95 corridor with D.C. and Virginia getting sheets of rain, heavy flooding in Maryland and New Jersey. Hours-long ground delays and ground stops at all three New York area airports and Philly, Baltimore and D.C. with delays building nationwide. Ethiopian 509, give way to Air India to disconnect on spot 26, you may disconnect on spot 27. Inside United Airlines Newark Ops Center, a tightly choreographed ballet. Newark is United's busiest East Coast hub, 760 departing and arriving flights every day. as we start to see the weather impacts start to build up out here to the west, so some of our west fixes are gonna get hit. just six weeks after Newark melted down due to air traffic control equipment failures and the loss of runway four left. Now reopened, the airport is running at full throttle. Newark shares this airspace with three other airports, major airports, JFK, LaGuardia, Teterboro. There's also 50 smaller airports nearby, and it makes this the most complicated airspace in the country. Athens, inbound 125, we'll have a medical emergency on board. ETA is 1356. Getting priority clearance, a United flight from Athens with a 25-year-old in medical distress. their aircraft taxiing is going to have to hold. And then the medical emergency is the first aircraft on the ground and to that gate. Amazingly, United says Newark is now the Big Apple's most on-time airport. Tonight, Mother Nature seems intent on ruining best laid plans. How well can we manage this tonight so tomorrow morning looks like nothing ever happened? Tom Costello, NBC News, Newark Airport.",
      "topic_subject": "Travel/Weather",
      "summary": "Correspondent Tom Costello reports from Newark Liberty International Airport on the severe travel disruptions caused by thunderstorms along the East Coast, detailing flight delays, cancellations, and the operational challenges faced by airlines.",
      "keywords": [
        "travel delays",
        "severe weather",
        "Newark Airport",
        "United Airlines",
        "flight cancellations",
        "Tom Costello"
      ]
    },
    {
      "start_time": 390.0,
      "end_time": 438.0,
      "transcript": "And the holiday travel colliding with a mounting public health crisis, closing beaches across the country just ahead of the 4th of July. Maggie Vespa is at a beach closed for swimming outside of Chicago. And Maggie, the timing couldn't be worse. Tom, exactly. At least 17 Chicago area beaches are closed tonight due to high bacteria levels. We're also tracking closures in at least five other states from New York to California. Of particular concern is Vibrio, a bacteria that occurs naturally in coastal waters and can spike amid extreme heat or heavy rain, causing serious, even potentially life-threatening infections in those who swallow the contaminated water or swim with open wounds. Public health officials pleading with people to heed these beach closures and adjust holiday plans as needed. Maggie Vespa with that alert tonight. Maggie, thank you.",
      "topic_subject": "Health/Environment",
      "summary": "A report on the closure of dozens of beaches across the U.S. due to high levels of bacteria, specifically Vibrio, posing health risks to swimmers during the holiday weekend.",
      "keywords": [
        "beach closures",
        "bacteria",
        "Vibrio",
        "public health",
        "Maggie Vespa"
      ]
    },
    {
      "start_time": 438.0,
      "end_time": 592.0,
      "transcript": "Now to Washington and that big victory for President Trump. His massive budget bill narrowly passing the Senate. But tonight there's still a divide among Republicans. Ryan Nobles is tracking it all for us. Tonight, the dramatic moment, Vice President Vance breaking a tie vote. The vice president votes in the affirmative. The bill as amended is passed. It isn't. In a major win for President Trump, the Senate. narrowly passing what he calls his big, beautiful bill, which extends the Trump tax cuts, increases border security, and eliminates taxes on tips and overtime. Thank you. Thank you. But three Republicans and every Democrat voting no. Republicans passed the biggest tax breaks for billionaires ever seen. Ultimately, it came down to Alaska Republican Lisa Murkowski, who negotiated a special carve-out for Alaska. Alaska to soften the blow of the changes to Medicaid, drawing the ire of Kentucky's Rand Paul, who voted no. They chose to add more pork and subsidies for Alaska to secure that. We pressed Murkowski. Senator Paul said that this was, that your vote was a bailout for Alaska at the expense of the rest of the country. That's what Senator Paul said. I'm just asking for your response. My response is, They have an obligation to the people of the state of Alaska. And I live up to that every single day. Now the bill goes back to the House. President Trump optimistic. Thank you. And responding to new criticism of the bill from Elon Musk, who says it's full of, quote, insane spending. The president posting, without subsidies, Elon would probably have to close up shop and head back home to South Africa. Doge is going to look at Musk, and if Doge looks at Musk, we're going to save a fortune. while Speaker Johnson insisting he can get the bill through the House again despite a razor-thin Republican majority. A lot of work ahead, but that's the job, so we'll get done. Ryan joins us now live from Capitol Hill. And Ryan, we just saw that moment in your piece with Senator Murkowski. And now you have new reporting about a Republican divide in the House. Yeah, that's right, Tom. House conservatives have said that they are unhappy with the changes that the Senate made to this bill. But GOP leaders are confident that with President Trump's help, they will get the votes they need to get this bill passed by Friday. Tom.",
      "topic_subject": "Politics",
      "summary": "Coverage of the Senate narrowly passing President Trump's budget bill, highlighting the tie-breaking vote by Vice President Vance, the political divide among Republicans, and the escalating feud between Trump and Elon Musk regarding government spending.",
      "keywords": [
        "Senate",
        "Trump",
        "budget bill",
        "Elon Musk",
        "Vice President Vance",
        "Lisa Murkowski"
      ]
    },
    {
      "start_time": 592.0,
      "end_time": 720.0,
      "transcript": "Ryan Nobles for us. And President Trump was in Florida to tour a new migrant detention center he's dubbed Alligator Alcatraz, calling it a blueprint to speed up his mass deportation efforts. Gabe Gutierrez is there. Deep in the Florida Everglades, tonight President Trump says this massive migrant detention camp built in just eight days has an unusual deterrent to prevent any escapes. Don't run in a straight line, run like this. He calls it Alligator Alcatraz. It's meant more as a joke, but the more I thought of it, the more I liked it. With gators clearly visible outside, NBC News getting a first look inside. with the state has been great. clients. I'm going to say that. What I would say is that, obviously, individuals that want to come here to enjoy the American dream. should think about returning on their own, so they're not in a detained setting. President Trump was elected pledging to deport the record number of migrants who crossed into the U.S. under President Biden. I think I'll say. According to data reviewed by NBC News, almost half of those currently in ICE custody have neither been convicted of nor charged with a crime. administration after the worst of the worst. administration stresses the priority is violent criminals, but anyone who is here the course of another law enforcement investigation, we do come across people that are just here illegally. We will no longer turn a blind eye to that. Get the hell out of our Everglades! The Department of Homeland Security the Biden administration. used to help. Gabe Gutierrez down in Miami for us, Gabe. We thank you. And when we return, we take you inside the fentanyl trade, tracking the supply chain from China to Fiji to America with DEA informants, our exclusive access. That's next. here.",
      "topic_subject": "Politics/Immigration",
      "summary": "President Trump tours a new migrant detention center in the Florida Everglades, nicknamed 'Alligator Alcatraz' due to the presence of alligators nearby, as part of his administration's deportation strategy.",
      "keywords": [
        "Trump",
        "Alligator Alcatraz",
        "migrant detention",
        "Florida",
        "Everglades",
        "deportation"
      ]
    },

The user wants me to segment the video based on changes in the verbal narrative's subject matter.

1.  **Analyze the video content:**
    *   **00:00 - 01:34:** Intro sequence with Tom Llamas previewing the top stories: Diddy trial, travel delays, beach closures, Trump's "Alligator Alcatraz", Trump's bill, and a DEA fentanyl sting.
    *   **01:34 - 04:05:** Detailed report on the Sean "Diddy" Combs trial verdict.
    *   **04:05 - 06:30:** Report on severe weather causing travel delays at airports.
    *   **06:30 - 07:18:** Report on beach closures due to high bacteria levels.
